message.abstract:
<jats:title>Structured Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To examine how VA shifted care delivery methods one year into the pandemic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>All encounters paid or provided by VA between January 1, 2019 and February 27, 2021.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We aggregated all VA paid or provided encounters and classified them into community (non-VA) acute and non-acute visits, VA acute and non-acute visits, and VA virtual visits. We then compared the number of encounters by week over time to pre-pandemic levels.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data Extraction Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Aggregation of administrative VA claims and health records.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Principal Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>VA has experienced a dramatic and persistent shift to providing virtual care and purchasing care from non-VA providers. Before the pandemic, a majority (63%) of VA care was provided in-person at a VA facility. One year into the pandemic, in-person care at VA’s constituted just 33% of all visits. Most of the difference made up by large expansions of virtual care; total VA provided visits (in person and virtual) declined (4.9 million to 4.2 million) while total visits of all types declined only 3.5%. Community provided visits exceeded prepandemic levels (2.3 million to 2.9 million, +26%).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Unlike private health care, VA has resumed in-person care slowly at its own facilities, and more rapidly in purchased care with different financial incentives a likely driver. The very large expansion of virtual care nearly made up the difference. With a widespread physical presence across the U.S., this has important implications for access to care and future allocation of medical personnel, facilities, and resources.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: The COVID-19 pandemic shifted the Veterans Affairs System toward being a payer and virtual care provider: is it sustainable?
